---
title: "Landslide Susceptibility in Santa Barbara County"
description: "An Interactive Map Displaying Calculated Landslide Risk In Santa Barbara"
author:
  - name: Ryan Green
date: 04/27/2026
categories: [Python, Landslide, Logistic Regression, Santa Barbara]
---


Introcution
--------

On January 9, 2018, a catastrophic debris flow struck Montecito, California,
killing 23 people and destroying more than 100 homes[^1]. The event was set in
motion by an intense rainstorm, delivering about half an inch of rainfall in
five minutes[^2], falling on steep slopes that had been almost entirely stripped
of vegetation just weeks earlier by the Thomas Fire. The fire burned nearly
282,000 acres across Ventura and Santa Barbara counties in late 2017[^3], leaving
behind bare soil that promoted runoff. The combination of steep terrain,
unstable geology, and the recent high-severity burn created the conditions for one of the
deadliest natural disasters in California history.

This project builds a county-wide landslide susceptibility model for Santa
Barbara County, attempting to capture the landscape conditions that make
slopes vulnerable, and tests the results on real recorded landslide locations.

[Open Interactive Map](susceptibility_interactive.html)


Approach: Logistic Regression
--------

The model uses logistic regression to calculate landslide risk based on landscape and climatic factors. It was trained on two types of locations: 926 confirmed landslide
sites drawn from the USGS National Landslide Inventory (used as "presences"),
and ~4,600 comparison points placed in areas at least 200 meters
away from any known landslide (used as "pseudo-absences"). By comparing the
landscape characteristics at these two groups of locations, the model learns
which combinations of terrain, climate, and land conditions are associated with
higher landslide likelihood.

The output is a probability score for every 10-meter pixel across the county, determining how closely each location resembles the conditions where landslides
have been recorded. These scores are grouped into five classes (Very Low through
Very High) based on where they fall in the county-wide distribution of all
land pixels: the bottom 30% become Very Low, the next 20% become Low, the next
20% Moderate, the next 15% High, and the top 15% Very High.

The Eight Factors
--------

Each factor captures a different physical condition that contributes to slope
instability. The influence percentage shown for each factor is how much that
factor contributed to the final model, based on its
statistical coefficient. All data sources are listed in the Citations and Data Sources section.

  Slope (27.5%)
  Steep terrain is the most consistently important predictor of landslide
  susceptibility in the scientific literature, and this model confirms that
  pattern. Slope is derived from a 10-meter digital elevation model.

  Precipitation (22.4%)
  Areas that receive more intense rainfall during extreme storm events are more
  likely to experience slope failure. This factor uses the 100-year, 24-hour
  storm depth from NOAA Atlas 14[^8], using the amount of rain expected in a single
  day in a very severe storm as a measure of the rainfall each respective 
  location recieves.

  Land Cover (17.1%)
  Vegetation helps stabilize slopes by anchoring soil with roots and absorbing
  rainfall. Bare ground, recently burned areas, and cleared or developed land
  provide much less stability. This factor uses a detailed ecosystem
  classification[^5] to score each land cover type by its associated risk level.

  Terrain Curvature (9.3%)
  Curvature describes the shape of the slope. Concave slopes tend to funnel water and debris, and are more
  often associated with landslide scour zones. This factor captures the subtle
  geometry of the landscape beyond just slope.

  Burn Severity (9.1%)
  Areas that have burned in recent years carry elevated landslide risk because
  fire removes vegetation, disrupts soil structure, and can create a water-
  repellent layer near the surface. This factor is calculated from CAL FIRE
  perimeter records[^11] and gives more weight to more recent fires using a three-year
  exponential decay to approximate how quickly slopes recover after fire.

  Lithology (7.6%)
  The underlying rock type affects how easily slope material weakens and fails.
  Marine shales, mudstones, and loose sedimentary deposits tend to absorb water
  and lose strength, making them more prone to failure. Hard crystalline rocks
  like granite are much more stable. Rock types are drawn from the USGS State
  Geologic Map Compilation.[^4]

  Soil Erodibility (6.7%)
  Some soils detach and move more easily than others under rainfall and runoff.
  This factor uses the K-factor from the USDA gSSURGO soil database[^7], which
  measures how readily a soil erodes. Higher erodibility is associated with
  higher landslide initiation potential.

  Vegetation Density -- NDVI (<1%)
  The Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) measures how much active,
  healthy vegetation is present at a location, as seen from satellite imagery[^6].
  Low NDVI values indicate sparse vegetation and reduced root reinforcement.
  In this model, NDVI contributed very little additional information beyond what
  the land cover factor already captured, so its influence on the final model
  is negligible.

Two additional factors that were initially considered, Topographic Wetness Index (TWI) and
geologic fault-line proximity, were left out of the final model. TWI
was too closely correlated with slope to add independent value. Fault proximity
describes where earthquakes might trigger landslides, which is a different
mechanism from the terrain and rainfall conditions this model is designed to
capture.

How the Map Is Made
--------

Each of the eight factors is processed into a raster, or a grid of values
covering the entire county, at 10-meter resolution. These are normalized to a
common scale (from 0 to 1) and fed into the logistic regression model, which combines
them into a single probability score for each pixel. That score is then
classified into five susceptibility classes, based on the percentile distribution
across all land pixels in the county.

The final map shows which parts of Santa Barbara County have landscape conditions
most similar to where landslides have historically occurred and been recorded.


How Well It Works
--------

The model was evaluated three ways:

  Cross-validation: The county was divided into seven geographic blocks. Each
  block was left out in turn while the model was trained on the other six, then
  tested on the withheld block. This tests whether the model generalizes to
  areas it was not trained on. The average AUC across all seven blocks was 0.719
  (where 1.0 is a perfect model, and 0.5 is no better than random guessing).
  The variation across blocks (+/- 0.256) was high, reflecting the fact that
  most training landslides are clustered in the Santa Ynez Mountains. Therefore, the
  model performs better in some parts of the county than others.

  Hold-out test: A random 20% of the 926 training landslide points were set
  aside before training. Of these 185 withheld points, 49.7% fell in the High
  or Very High susceptibility class.

  Independent validation: 8,323 landslide points mapped after the January 9,
  2023 atmospheric river storm in the Santa Ynez Mountains, which were compared against the model
  output. This data was not used in model training. 92.8% of those points fell in the High or Very High class. This is
  the strongest indicator of model performance, because the validation data
  comes from a real storm event.


Interactive Map
--------

This project outputs an interactive map of Santa Barbara County, displaying the
susceptibility raster alongside several additional data layers: historical
landslide deposit polygons from the USGS National Landslide Inventory (1,043
confirmed events)[^9], the 926 training landslide points used to build the model,
Quaternary fault lines with slip-rate and age attributes[^10], CAL FIRE fire
perimeters since 2016[^11], and surface geology[^4]. An address search tool allows
users to look up the risk score and the leading contributing factors for
any address in the county.


------------------------------------------------------------------------

LIMITATIONS
-----------

1. Training data is unevenly distributed across the county.
   The 926 training landslide locations are heavily concentrated in the Santa
   Ynez Mountains, because that is where most historical landslide mapping has
   been done. In other parts of the county, the absence of mapped landslides reflects gaps in the inventory rather than a genuinely lower rate of
   slope failure. The model may underestimate risk in areas with little mapping
   coverage.

2. The model is static.
   All input layers represent conditions at a fixed point in time. The model
   does not automatically update when vegetation recovers after a fire, when
   seasonal soil moisture changes, or when new fires occur. The burn severity
   factor is recalculated from historical fire perimeters, but it is not a
   real-time feed.

3. The model identifies susceptibility, not probability of failure.
   A pixel classified as High does not have a quantified probability of failing
   in any given storm. The model identifies where landscape conditions resemble
   those of past landslide locations, and cannot say if a failure will occur
   or how large it will be.

4. No triggering events are included.
   Landslides require both a susceptible landscape and something to set them
   off, whether it be intense rainfall, an earthquake, etc. This model
   captures only the inherent terrain conditions. Two
   areas with the same susceptibility score may have very different actual
   failure histories depending on the storms or earthquakes they have
   experienced.

5. Geology and land cover boundaries can create abrupt transitions.
   Because geology and land cover are derived from polygon maps converted to
   a raster grid, susceptibility scores can jump sharply at their boundaries.
   A single pixel on one side of a geology boundary might score very differently
   from the pixel next to it, even though no abrupt physical change in the
   landscape actually exists there.

6. The validation datasets have their own limitations.
   The NLI hold-out test uses points from the same inventory used for training,
   which means any gaps or biases in the NLI affect both sides of
   the evaluation. The Santa Ynez 2023 validation is geographically
   restricted to one storm event in one part of the county, so it may not
   represent model performance across the full range of landscape conditions.
   Absence of a landslide polygon in the inventory does not mean a slope has
   never failed there.

7. Historical landslide polygons have variable location accuracy.
   The USGS NLI polygons displayed in the map range from "Possible" to
   "High confidence" in accuracy, and many were mapped from aerial or satellite
   imagery rather than field survey. They should not be treated as precise
   failure boundaries.

8. Fine-scale features are not captured.
   At 10-meter pixel spacing, small-scale topographic features that may strongly
   control where failures begin, such as narrow gullies, tension cracks, or small scarps, are not resolved. Susceptibility in localized steep terrain may be
   underestimated relative to the surrounding area.


------------------------------------------------------------------------

CITATIONS AND DATA SOURCES
---------------------------

Cited Works

[^1]:  Kean, J.W., et al. (2019). "Inundation, flow dynamics, and damage in the
     9 January 2018 Montecito debris-flow event, California, USA."
     Geosphere, 15(4): 1140-1163.
     https://doi.org/10.1130/GES02040.1

[^2]:  Oakley, N.S., et al. (2018). "Brief communication: Meteorological and
     climatological conditions associated with the 9 January 2018 post-fire
     debris flows in Montecito and Carpinteria, California, USA."
     Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences, 18: 3037-3043.
     https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/18/3037/2018/

[^3]:  Oakley et al. (2018). Same as [2]. The Thomas Fire burned 281,893 acres
     across Ventura and Santa Barbara Counties, December 4, 2017 through
     January 12, 2018.

[^12]: Thomas, M.A., et al. (2025). "Landslide, soil, and vegetation
     measurements following an atmospheric river storm on January 9, 2023,
     in the Santa Ynez Mountains, California, USA." U.S. Geological Survey
     data release.
     https://doi.org/10.5066/P133CHYQ


Data Sources

[^4]:  Horton, J.D., San Juan, C.A., and Stoeser, D.B. (2017). USGS State
     Geologic Map Compilation (SGMC) -- California geology layer.
     U.S. Geological Survey Data Series 1052.
     https://www.usgs.gov/data/state-geologic-map-compilation-sgmc-geodatabase-conterminous-united-states

[^5]:  U.S. Geological Survey. GAP/LANDFIRE National Terrestrial Ecosystems
     2011 -- land cover classification.
     https://www.usgs.gov/data/gaplandfire-national-terrestrial-ecosystems-2011

[^6]:  European Space Agency. Sentinel-2 satellite imagery -- used to derive
     NDVI via Microsoft Planetary Computer.
     https://www.esa.int/Applications/Observing_the_Earth/Copernicus/Sentinel-2

[^7]:  USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service. Gridded Soil Survey
     Geographic (gSSURGO) Database -- soil erodibility (K-factor).
     https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/resources/data-and-reports/gridded-soil-survey-geographic-gssurgo-database

[^8]:  NOAA Hydrometeorological Design Studies Center. NOAA Atlas 14
     Precipitation Frequency Data Server -- 100-year/24-hour storm depth.
     https://hdsc.nws.noaa.gov/pfds/

[^9]:  U.S. Geological Survey. Landslide Inventories across the United States,
     ver. 3.0, February 2025 -- training and display landslide polygons.
     https://www.usgs.gov/data/landslide-inventories-across-united-states-ver-30-february-2025

[^10]: U.S. Geological Survey. Quaternary Fault and Fold Database of the
     United States -- fault lines display layer.
     https://www.usgs.gov/programs/earthquake-hazards/faults

[^11]: CAL FIRE Fire and Resource Assessment Program. California Fire Perimeters
     (all) -- burn severity and fire history display layer.
     https://data.ca.gov/dataset/california-fire-perimeters-all

[^13]: U.S. Geological Survey, 3D Elevation Program (3DEP). 1/3 arc-second
     National Elevation Dataset -- digital elevation model.
     https://www.usgs.gov/3d-elevation-program

[^14]: Macrostrat. Geologic map API -- supplemental geology attributes.
     https://macrostrat.org/api/

[^15]: U.S. Census Bureau. TIGER/Line Shapefiles -- Santa Barbara County
     administrative boundary.
     https://www.census.gov/geographies/mapping-files/time-series/geo/tiger-line-file.html
